topic: An employee is asked to train their replacement
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the conference room hummed with an irritating monotony. Sarah sat across the polished mahogany table, her hands gripping the edge of her notepad so tightly her knuckles were white. Mark, the department head, leaned forward, his expression unreadable. "So, Sarah," he began, his voice smooth and measured, "I need you to onboard Alex. You'll be his primary mentor for the next six weeks." Sarah blinked slowly, taking in the sheer volume of responsibility suddenly settling on her shoulders. A faint tremor ran through her hands. She tried to formulate a coherent response, but the words caught in her throat. *Train him? Me?* The air felt suddenly too thin. She just nodded, a small, jerky movement, trying to process the instruction while simultaneously calculating how she was going to survive the next few months.

[story 2]

"This is a big step, Liam," Maria said, gesturing toward the stack of onboarding manuals. Her tone was encouraging, almost too bright. Liam stared at the documents, the typeface swimming before his eyes. He shifted in his chair, the cheap plastic squeaking in protest. He felt a strange lightness in his chest, followed immediately by a heavy, sinking feeling in his stomach. He looked up at Maria, his gaze unfocused. "I... I don't know where to start," he managed, his voice coming out reedy and uncertain. He picked up a pen, but it felt alien in his grip. Every potential scenario—the inevitable mistakes, the awkward silences, the sheer volume of new information—spun into a dizzying, chaotic whirl behind his eyes. He just wanted to retreat.

[story 3]

The office smelled sharply of stale coffee and new plastic. David stood by the window, watching the city traffic flow below, an odd detachment settling over him. When his manager announced the training assignment, David simply tilted his head, processing the information at a glacial pace. He didn't speak immediately. Instead, he began meticulously straightening the papers on his desk, smoothing out wrinkles that weren't there. His movements were precise, almost obsessive. He could feel a distinct pressure building behind his temples, a kind of internal static. *I have to teach someone else how to do my job,* he thought, the realization landing with a dull thud. He finally turned, meeting his manager’s eyes, a look of profound uncertainty etched onto his face.
